Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Deterministic Allocation via Hessian Conditioning
Short Hypothesis: The local curvature of the loss landscape, quantified by the Hessian condition number, provides a deterministic proxy for precision requirements in mixed-precision inference, enabling stable, resource-efficient routing without heuristic calibration.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision frameworks rely on static profiling (e.g., NVIDIA TensorRT) or heuristic sensitivity analysis (e.g., Mixed Precision Training by Micikevicius et al.), which fail to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Recent training-free approaches leverage activation geometry (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing) but overlook explicit links to numerical stability. Our proposal uniquely grounds precision allocation in Hessian conditioning, providing formal stability guarantees and deterministic execution pathways.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant computational savings but often relies on heuristic calibration or static profiling, limiting adaptability and stability under distribution shifts. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on the local curvature of the loss landscape. We hypothesize that the Hessian condition number serves as a direct proxy for numerical stability, with ill-conditioned regions requiring higher precision to prevent catastrophic degradation. CAPR computes Hessian-vector products on-the-fly using randomized Lanczos iterations, mapping condition numbers to precision tiers under strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds linking Hessian conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii, CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate CAPR on WILDS benchmarks (e.g., Camelyon17, CivilComments) under natural distribution shifts, comparing throughput, accuracy, and stability against static mixed-precision baselines.
Validate the Hessian condition number as a proxy for precision sensitivity by measuring accuracy degradation under varying condition numbers and precision tiers.
Assess computational overhead of on-the-fly Hessian-vector product computation using randomized Lanczos iterations, ensuring strict latency budgets are met.
Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of Hessian conditioning versus other geometric diagnostics (e.g., activation covariance) on precision allocation.
Evaluate PAC-Bayes generalization bounds under quantization noise, verifying theoretical claims linking Hessian conditioning to stability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Hessian-vector product computation may introduce overhead, limiting applicability to latency-critical applications.
Randomized Lanczos iterations may fail to converge for highly ill-conditioned matrices, requiring fallback mechanisms.
Theoretical bounds may be overly conservative, leading to suboptimal precision allocation in practice.
The framework assumes access to a differentiable loss function, limiting applicability to black-box models.
